summary: Controls the SMBv1 server protocol value for LanmanServer. Microsoft directly documents the same LanmanServer registry path, value name, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for this setting.

setting.name: SMBv1 Server Protocol Support
setting.casual_explanation: SMB is the file-sharing protocol Windows uses for network shares. SMBv1 is the old version. Modern systems should avoid it, but some very old devices still need it.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable SMBv1 for security and to modernize the network stack. They only keep it enabled when they still have old devices or software that cannot speak SMBv2 or SMBv3.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling SMBv1 is usually safer, but any device that only understands SMBv1 may stop working. This record only documents the LanmanServer registry value and not every optional feature or client-side component that can also affect SMBv1 behavior.
setting.professional_notes: The documented app mapping covers HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters\SMB1. SMBv1 availability can still depend on Windows version, optional feature state, and whether the server component is installed.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed default
allowed_values.meaning: Windows decides behavior based on version, installed features, and component defaults.

allowed_values.label: Disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The SMBv1 server protocol is disabled for LanmanServer.

allowed_values.label: Enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The SMBv1 server protocol is enabled for LanmanServer.

targets.notes: This target does not by itself describe every SMBv1-related client or feature switch in Windows.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes SMB1 = 0 for the tweak, which matches the documented disable behavior for the LanmanServer registry value.

windows_defaults.label: Version-dependent Windows baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10, Windows 11, and Windows Server families

states.rationale: SMBv1 defaults vary by Windows version and feature installation state.

windows_defaults.notes: This is intentionally not collapsed into a single number because Microsoft treats SMBv1 defaults differently across client and server generations.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows managed default
intended_for: Mixed environments where SMBv1 requirements are still unknown
avoid_for: Hardening baselines that explicitly ban SMBv1

states.rationale: Leave the explicit registry override absent and let Windows manage the baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You are still auditing old devices or do not want the app to force a server-side SMBv1 stance yet.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You already know your environment should not expose SMBv1.
tradeoffs: Behavior may vary by Windows version and feature state.
This is less explicit than a hard disable.

recommended_profiles.label: Secure modern network
intended_for: Most home users with modern devices

states.rationale: Explicitly disables the SMBv1 server protocol.

recommended_profiles.good_when: Everything on the network already supports SMBv2 or SMBv3.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You still rely on legacy file-sharing equipment.
tradeoffs: Some old devices or shares may stop working.
Security posture is usually better on modern networks.

recommended_profiles.label: Legacy compatibility
intended_for: Old devices that only speak SMBv1

states.rationale: Explicitly enables the SMBv1 server protocol for compatibility.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You have confirmed that a required device or workflow still depends on SMBv1.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are enabling it just in case and do not know whether you really need it.

evidence.title: Microsoft SMBv1/v2/v3 detection and enable or disable guidance
evidence.summary: Official Microsoft guidance for detecting and enabling or disabling SMBv1, including the LanmanServer SMB1 registry value.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes SMB1 = 0 under LanmanServer for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: Current repo provenance already links this tweak to nohuto references such as win-config/network/desc.md and win-registry/records/LanmanServer.txt.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters -> SMB1, including 0 for Disabled and 1 for Enabled. The app writes that same documented value on the documented server-side SMBv1 path.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: To enable or disable SMBv1 on an SMB server, open Registry Editor and go to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters. Name: SMB1. Type: REG_DWORD. Data: 0 for Disabled and 1 for Enabled.
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft documents the same LanmanServer registry path, value name, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for the server-side SMBv1 override.
